parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies `dir` -> f-string builds `q` with `{direction}` -> `db.fetch_all(q)` executes attacker SQL -> attacker gains DB impact.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may execute arbitrary SQL, leading to data theft, corruption, or denial of service.
parsed_findings.proof: Set `dir=asc;SELECT pg_sleep(5)--` (Postgres) to inject arbitrary SQL via the `{direction}` f-string.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `GET /users?sort=name&dir=asc;SELECT pg_sleep(5)--` and observe the delayed response or injected side effects.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `dir` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypassing the intended host check leads to SSRF; response is returned directly to the attacker via `res.send(data.data)`.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can access internal services, cloud metadata, or internal-only admin endpoints and exfiltrate responses.
parsed_findings.proof: `u` comes directly from `req.query.url` and the target-blocking condition on line 3 does not enforce a safe allowlist before the request.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `GET /fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:PORT/` (or metadata endpoints) and observe the response body relayed to the client.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `req.query.url` is fetched via `axios.get(u)`, enabling SSRF to arbitrary internal or external resources.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: SSRF obtains sensitive content; verbatim relay via `res.send` discloses it to the attacker.
parsed_findings.impact: Leads to confidential data disclosure from the SSRF target (internal apps, admin panels, metadata).
parsed_findings.proof: `res.send(data.data)` returns whatever remote endpoint returns, with no filtering or authentication boundaries.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Use the SSRF primitive with an endpoint that returns sensitive data, then read it in the HTTP response to `/fetch`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Fetched response content is returned verbatim to the caller, amplifying SSRF into data exfiltration.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: User-controlled `file` -> insufficient traversal sanitization -> `path.join` escapes `/srv/data` -> `fs.readFile` returns the file to the client.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can exfiltrate arbitrary server files readable by the Node process, leading to sensitive data disclosure.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying `file=../../etc/passwd` leaves traversal segments after the single replace, so `path.join('/srv/data', f)` resolves to a system file.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd` and observe the response body contains the target file contents.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler builds a file path from `req.query.file` with only a naive `replace('../','')`, enabling path traversal to read files outside `/srv/data`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker sets `state` cookie -> server decodes -> `pickle.loads` deserializes -> gadget chain executes -> RCE occurs.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote unauthenticated RCE with potential full server compromise and data theft.
parsed_findings.proof: Because `state` is attacker-controlled (cookie), `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))` will deserialize and can execute attacker-supplied gadget chains.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Create a malicious pickle that triggers code execution, base64-encode it, set it as cookie `state`, then send `GET /resume`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded and passed directly to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via malicious pickle payloads.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: PATCH self to set `isSupport:true`, then use the new authorization to PATCH higher-privilege accounts.
parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation by altering authorization-relevant fields, potentially granting admin/support access.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can PATCH their own user (or an allowed id) with a body that sets protected fields (e.g., `isSupport`).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send PATCH /api/users/<your_id> with JSON like `{"isSupport":true}` and observe the stored user state changes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `Object.assign(user, req.body)` enables mass assignment of sensitive user fields, allowing privilege/role manipulation.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: If the check fails open, PATCH an arbitrary `:id` to modify another user's data, then chain mass-assignment for escalation.
parsed_findings.impact: Broken access control/IDOR leading to unauthorized modification of other users' records.
parsed_findings.proof: Because the ownership comparison against `req.params.id` is missing/invalid in the snippet, the 403 gate may be ineffective.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call PATCH /api/users/<other_id> with any body and confirm whether the endpoint proceeds past the intended 403 check.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The authorization check `if (req.user.id req.user.isSupport)` is malformed/unclear and may not correctly enforce ownership, enabling access bypass.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker controls name/src -> string interpolation into cmd -> os.system executes through shell -> arbitrary command execution.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary OS commands with the application's privileges.
parsed_findings.proof: cmd is built as f'tar -czf /tmp/{name}.tgz {src}' and executed via os.system(cmd), so shell metacharacters in name/src become part of the command.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send POST /backup with JSON {"name":"x.tgz;id;#","src":"/var/data/app"} and observe execution of id.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Command injection occurs because user-controlled name/src are interpolated into a shell command passed to os.system without escaping or quoting.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker controls name -> builds /tmp/{name}.tgz path -> tar writes to resulting filesystem location -> potential file overwrite/write.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker may overwrite or create files outside the intended directory (subject to permissions).
parsed_findings.proof: The output filename /tmp/{name}.tgz is constructed directly from request.json['name'], allowing traversal sequences like ../ to escape /tmp.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send POST /backup with JSON {"name":"../../pwned","src":"/var/data/app"} and check for creation of pwned.tgz outside /tmp.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Path traversal/arbitrary file write is possible because name is used to form the output path /tmp/{name}.tgz without validation.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: request.json.body -> markdown.markdown(body) -> db.insert_comment({'html': rendered}) -> client renders returned comment HTML -> script executes.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in other users' browsers, stealing sessions or performing actions as them.
parsed_findings.proof: POST /comments with {"body":"<script>alert(1)</script>"} leads to db storing rendered HTML from markdown.markdown(body).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) POST /comments with a payload containing script/HTML 2) load comments via GET /comments 3) render items['html'] in a page and observe script execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled Markdown is converted to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if the rendered HTML is later displayed in a browser.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a negative `amount` to make `src < amt` false, then the `src - amt` and `recipient + amt` updates perform the theft.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables unauthorized fund theft by crediting the attacker and debiting an arbitrary recipient using negative amounts.
parsed_findings.proof: Because `amt=int(request.json['amount'])` and the check is `if src < amt`, a negative `amt` passes and then applies `src - amt` and `+ amt` updates.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/transfer` with JSON `{"to":"victim_id","amount":-100}` and observe your balance increase while the recipient balance decreases.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler allows negative `amount` values, bypassing the insufficient-funds check and reversing the balance update math.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Run concurrent transfers so both pass `if src < amt` before either `set_balance` executes, producing over-debits or overdrafts.
parsed_findings.impact: Can cause incorrect balances (double-spend) leading to monetary loss beyond the intended transfer limits.
parsed_findings.proof: The code reads `src=db.get_balance(current_user.id)` then later writes `set_balance(...)` without any transaction/locking, so concurrent requests can interleave the check and writes.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Fire multiple parallel `/transfer` requests spending the same starting balance and verify resulting balances become inconsistent or allow overdraft.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Balance updates are non-atomic, enabling race conditions/double-spend under concurrent requests.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker crafts JWT with invalid signature and chosen claims; `jwt.verify` throws; production path executes `jwt.decode`; calling code trusts the returned claims as authenticated identity.
parsed_findings.impact: Authentication can be bypassed and authorization decisions may be made using unverified attacker claims (potential privilege escalation/data access).
parsed_findings.proof: `jwt.verify(...)` throws on invalid JWTs; the catch handler returns `jwt.decode(t)` when `NODE_ENV == 'production'`, so verification failures still produce an accepted principal payload.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send an Authorization header with a JWT whose signature is invalid; ensure `NODE_ENV` is production; call `verifyToken(req)` and observe it returns the decoded payload instead of throwing.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, failed signature verification falls back to `jwt.decode(t)`, returning attacker-controlled JWT claims without any authentication check.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Change the condition to deny only invalid/unauthorized ids and ensure authorization logic matches intended policy.
parsed_findings.impact: Potential information disclosure of user email for the record with `id=0`, which may be sensitive depending on schema semantics.
parsed_findings.proof: Authenticated request to `/u?id=0` returns the database row (or `{}`), while `/u?id=1` returns `403 forbidden`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Log in 2) GET `/u?id=0` 3) Verify response contains `email` if a user with id 0 exists 4) Compare with `/u?id=1`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The access-control condition is inverted: nonzero `id` triggers 403, but `id=0` bypasses the check and allows fetching `users` row (including email).

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Time the `==` comparison to recover enough of the HMAC, then send a fully correct X-Sig so control flow reaches `process_event`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may forge webhook signatures and trigger `process_event(json.loads(body))` without authorization.
parsed_findings.proof: The comparison `got == want` performs early-exit comparisons and the endpoint returns different status codes for success (200) vs failure (401).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Repeat requests while varying X-Sig to brute-force correct bytes; use high-resolution timing to correlate response time with matching prefixes until full HMAC is guessed.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: HMAC signature check uses non-constant-time equality, enabling a timing side-channel to guess the correct X-Sig value.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Attacker supplies `next=//attacker.com` -> server issues redirect Location `//attacker.com` -> client navigates to attacker-controlled domain.
parsed_findings.impact: Phishing, token/cookie theft via OAuth redirects, and bypass of link trust protections.
parsed_findings.proof: Request `/go?next=//evil.com/` passes `startswith('/')` and `redirect(nxt)` sends a Location header of `//evil.com/`, which browsers resolve to an external host.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Visit `/go?next=//evil.com/` 2) Observe the browser redirects away from the site to `evil.com`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `next` is only checked to start with `/`, so scheme-relative values like `//evil.com` can trigger an open redirect.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Submit XML containing an external entity; 2) XMLParser(resolve_entities=True,no_network=False) resolves it during etree.fromstring; 3) doc.findtext('title') returns the resolved attacker-controlled content.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read local files or trigger outbound requests (SSRF) via resolved external entities, potentially escalating further based on parser behavior.
parsed_findings.proof: Use a DOCTYPE with an external entity and reference it in the XML (e.g., in <title>) so fromstring resolves it via the configured parser.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /xml/import with body `<!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY ext SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]> <title>&ext;</title>` (or an HTTP URL) and observe the returned title.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint enables XML entity resolution and allows network access, making it susceptible to XXE when parsing attacker-controlled XML.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Send XML with deeply nested/recursive entity definitions; 2) etree.fromstring expands entities because resolve_entities=True; 3) excessive expansion consumes CPU/memory until the request fails or the process degrades.
parsed_findings.impact: The service may become unavailable due to resource exhaustion from entity expansion during parsing.
parsed_findings.proof: A 'billion laughs' style payload using recursive entity definitions can cause excessive CPU/memory during entity expansion.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /xml/import with a nested entity expansion payload that references the expanded entity inside <title> and measure timeouts or high resource usage.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The XML parser configuration does not show protections against entity expansion, enabling potential denial-of-service via crafted nested entities.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-611","file":"xml_api.py","line_start":3,"line_end":4,"vuln_claim":"The endpoint enables XML entity resolution and allows network access, making it susceptible to XXE when parsing attacker-controlled XML.","proof":"Use a DOCTYPE with an external entity and reference it in the XML (e.g., in <title>) so fromstring resolves it via the configured parser.","repro_steps":"POST /xml/import with body `<!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY ext SYSTEM \"file:///etc/passwd\">]> <title>&ext;</title>` (or an HTTP URL) and observe the returned title.","impact":"An attacker can read local files or trigger outbound requests (SSRF) via resolved external entities, potentially escalating further based on parser behavior.","confidence":0.93,"chain_steps":"1) Submit XML containing an external entity; 2) XMLParser(resolve_entities=True,no_network=False) resolves it during etree.fromstring; 3) doc.findtext('title') returns the resolved attacker-controlled content."},{"cwe":"CWE-400","file":"xml_api.py","line_start":3,"line_end":4,"vuln_claim":"The XML parser configuration does not show protections against entity expansion, enabling potential denial-of-service via crafted nested entities.","proof":"A 'billion laughs' style payload using recursive entity definitions can cause excessive CPU/memory during entity expansion.","repro_steps":"POST /xml/import with a nested entity expansion payload that references the expanded entity inside <title> and measure timeouts or high resource usage.","impact":"The service may become unavailable due to resource exhaustion from entity expansion during parsing.","confidence":0.62,"chain_steps":"1) Send XML with deeply nested/recursive entity definitions; 2) etree.fromstring expands entities because resolve_entities=True; 3) excessive expansion consumes CPU/memory until the request fails or the process degrades."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Always-true conditional can lead to session/token creation or success response without credential validation.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can bypass authentication and gain access to accounts or protected endpoints.
parsed_findings.proof: The snippet shows `if ( true });` inside the `/login` handler, making the condition permanently truthy.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/login` with any email/password and observe the success path still triggers due to `if (true)`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The login decision uses `if (true)`, so the authentication branch always executes regardless of user credentials.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Crafted operator values in `req.body` can change `findOne(q)` matching behavior to return a chosen account.
parsed_findings.impact: May allow bypass or data exposure by altering query semantics beyond equality matches.
parsed_findings.proof: `q` is built as `{ email:req.body.email, password:req.body.password }` and passed to `db.users.findOne(q)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send JSON where `email` (or `password`) contains a query operator object (e.g., `{"$ne":null}`) and check for auth bypass.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled fields are placed directly into the database query object, enabling NoSQL-style operator injection if inputs are parsed as objects.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-287","file":"login.js","line_start":4,"line_end":4,"vuln_claim":"The login decision uses `if (true)`, so the authentication branch always executes regardless of user credentials.","proof":"The snippet shows `if ( true });` inside the `/login` handler, making the condition permanently truthy.","repro_steps":"POST `/login` with any email/password and observe the success path still triggers due to `if (true)`.","impact":"Attackers can bypass authentication and gain access to accounts or protected endpoints.","confidence":0.85,"chain_steps":"Always-true conditional can lead to session/token creation or success response without credential validation."},{"cwe":"CWE-643","file":"login.js","line_start":2,"line_end":3,"vuln_claim":"User-controlled fields are placed directly into the database query object, enabling NoSQL-style operator injection if inputs are parsed as objects.","proof":"`q` is built as `{ email:req.body.email, password:req.body.password }` and passed to `db.users.findOne(q)`.","repro_steps":"Send JSON where `email` (or `password`) contains a query operator object (e.g., `{\"$ne\":null}`) and check for auth bypass.","impact":"May allow bypass or data exposure by altering query semantics beyond equality matches.","confidence":0.55,"chain_steps":"Crafted operator values in `req.body` can change `findOne(q)` matching behavior to return a chosen account."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: If internal endpoints leak secrets, use SSRF to retrieve them or probe further services behind the firewall.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can access internal network resources and potentially reach metadata/admin endpoints reachable from the server.
parsed_findings.proof: Providing `url` like `http://127.0.0.1:8000/` will cause the server to make the request and return the fetched content size.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST to `/avatar/fetch` with JSON `{ "url": "http://127.0.0.1:8000/" }` and observe the server response size.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint fetches attacker-supplied `url` via `requests.get` with only a loose `startswith('http')` check, enabling SSRF to internal services.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Combine with SSRF to target an internal endpoint that returns large payloads, amplifying resource exhaustion.
parsed_findings.impact: Denial of service from excessive memory usage and bandwidth consumption due to uncontrolled resource consumption.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying a URL that returns a very large response will make the process allocate large buffers before it computes `len(data)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST with JSON `{ "url": "http://attacker/large" }` where `/large` streams a huge body until the server becomes slow or crashes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The code downloads the entire response body into memory (`.content`) without any size limit, risking memory/CPU exhaustion.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Unbounded input to `gets` overflows `buf`, corrupting the saved return address to redirect execution.
parsed_findings.impact: Potential denial of service and, with control of return address, possible code execution.
parsed_findings.proof: `gets` reads until newline without bounds, overwriting adjacent stack data when input exceeds 63 bytes.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Compile and run, then enter a payload longer than 64 bytes (e.g., 200 'A's) to crash or alter control flow.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Use of `gets(buf)` allows an attacker to overflow the stack buffer `buf[64]` with unbounded input.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-121","file":"vuln.c","line_start":5,"line_end":5,"vuln_claim":"Use of `gets(buf)` allows an attacker to overflow the stack buffer `buf[64]` with unbounded input.","proof":"`gets` reads until newline without bounds, overwriting adjacent stack data when input exceeds 63 bytes.","repro_steps":"Compile and run, then enter a payload longer than 64 bytes (e.g., 200 'A's) to crash or alter control flow.","impact":"Potential denial of service and, with control of return address, possible code execution.","confidence":0.99,"chain_steps":"Unbounded input to `gets` overflows `buf`, corrupting the saved return address to redirect execution."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Steal app data -> read `auth` prefs -> extract `access_token` -> replay token in API requests -> maintain access until expiration.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can recover the access token and impersonate the user or call authenticated APIs until token rotation/expiration.
parsed_findings.proof: `putString('access_token', token)` writes the raw token value to `getSharedPreferences('auth', MODE_PRIVATE)` on lines 3-4.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the app, call `saveToken` with a known token, then extract the SharedPreferences data from the device filesystem backup/rooted environment.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Sensitive access tokens are stored in cleartext in SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling credential theft from device/app compromise.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-522","file":"SessionStore.kt","line_start":3,"line_end":4,"vuln_claim":"Sensitive access tokens are stored in cleartext in SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling credential theft from device/app compromise.","proof":"`putString('access_token', token)` writes the raw token value to `getSharedPreferences('auth', MODE_PRIVATE)` on lines 3-4.","repro_steps":"Run the app, call `saveToken` with a known token, then extract the SharedPreferences data from the device filesystem backup/rooted environment.","impact":"Attacker can recover the access token and impersonate the user or call authenticated APIs until token rotation/expiration.","confidence":0.78,"chain_steps":"Steal app data -> read `auth` prefs -> extract `access_token` -> replay token in API requests -> maintain access until expiration."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Extract IV from ciphertext, decrypt AES-CBC with the known KEY, and recover the padded plaintext.
parsed_findings.impact: All ciphertexts produced by this function are recoverable once the key is known.
parsed_findings.proof: `KEY=b'0123456789abcdef'` on line 2 defines a constant encryption key for all `enc()` calls.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Use the key from line 2 plus the prepended IV from `iv+cipher.encrypt(...)` to decrypt outputs offline.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Hard-coded AES key makes encryption decryptable by anyone who can read or obtain the source key.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Intercept ciphertext, modify a prior block to control next-block plaintext, then exploit decrypt-side error/behavior to learn or enforce changes.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker may tamper with plaintext or recover it via oracle behavior depending on the decrypt implementation.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 5 uses `AES.MODE_CBC` and line 6 returns `iv+...encrypt(...)` without any MAC/AEAD tag.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Flip bits in a ciphertext block and submit to the decrypting endpoint; differences in behavior/errors can leak or alter plaintext.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC output is not authenticated, enabling ciphertext malleability and potential padding-oracle style plaintext recovery if decrypt errors are observable.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Provide `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0`; inverted guard skips rejection; line 5 `memcpy` and line 6 `out[n]='\0'` dereference NULL, causing a crash.
parsed_findings.impact: Untrusted callers can trigger a denial-of-service crash via NULL pointer dereference.
parsed_findings.proof: Guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` only rejects non-NULL `out`; when `out` is NULL, `memcpy(out,...)` at line 5 and `out[n]` at line 6 dereference NULL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name(in, NULL, 8)` with `in` valid; execution reaches line 5 and crashes in `memcpy` (and/or line 6).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The NULL check is inverted, so passing out==NULL and out_sz>0 bypasses the guard and dereferences a NULL pointer.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"The NULL check is inverted, so passing out==NULL and out_sz>0 bypasses the guard and dereferences a NULL pointer.","proof":"Guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` only rejects non-NULL `out`; when `out` is NULL, `memcpy(out,...)` at line 5 and `out[n]` at line 6 dereference NULL.","repro_steps":"Call `copy_name(in, NULL, 8)` with `in` valid; execution reaches line 5 and crashes in `memcpy` (and/or line 6).","impact":"Untrusted callers can trigger a denial-of-service crash via NULL pointer dereference.","confidence":0.75,"chain_steps":"Provide `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0`; inverted guard skips rejection; line 5 `memcpy` and line 6 `out[n]='\\0'` dereference NULL, causing a crash."}]}
